prologue.head: The TragicalTragicall History of the Life and Death of Doctor Faustus (1616)

sp.l: Not marching in the fields of ThrasimeneThrasimen,
Where Mars did mate the warlikewarlicke Carthagens,
Nor sporting in the dalliance of loveloue
In courtsCourts of kingsKings, where state is overturnedouer-turn'd,
Nor in the pomppompe of proud audacious deeds,
Intends our Muse to vaunt his heavenlyheauenly verse.
OnlyOnely this, gentlesGentles: we must now performperforme
The formforme of Faustus' fortunes, good or bad.,
And now to patient judgmentsiudgements we appealappeale,
And speakspeake for Faustus in his infancyinfancie.
Now is he bornborne, of parents base of stockstocke,
In Germany, within a townTowne calledcal'd Rhodes.:
At riper yearsyeares to Wittenberg he went,
Whereas his kinsmen chiefly brought him upvp.;
So much he profits in divinityDiuinitie,
That shortly he was gracedgrac'd with Doctor'sDoctors name,
Excelling all, and sweetly can dispute
In th'heavenlyth'heauenly matters of theologyTheologie.,
Till swoll'nswolne with cunning, of a selfselfe conceit,
His waxen wings did mount aboveaboue his reach
And melting, heavensheauens conspiredconspir'd his overthrowouer-throw,:
For falling to a devilishdiuellish exercise,
And glutted now with learning'slearnings golden gifts,
He surfeitssurfets uponvpon cursed necromancyNecromancie.:
Nothing so sweet as magicMagicke is to him;
Which he preferspreferres before his chiefest blissblisse,
And this the man that in his study sits.
Settle thy studies Faustus, and begin
to sound the depth of that thou wilt professprofesse.,
HavingHauing commencedcommenc'd, be a divineDiuine in showshew,
Yet levelleuell at the end of everyeuery artArt,
And liveliue and die in Aristotle'sAristotles worksworkes.
Sweet AnalyticsAnalitikes, 'tistis thou hast ravishedrauisht me.,
Bene disserere est finis logicesLogicis.
Is to dispute well logic'sLogickes chiefest end?
AffordsAffoords this artArt no greater miracle?
Then read no more;, thou hast attainedattain'd that end.;
A greater subject fitteth Faustus' wit.:
Bid economyOeconomy farewell,; and Galen come.:
Be a physicianPhisitian, Faustus;, heapheape upvp gold,
And be eternizedeterniz'd for some wondrous cure.:
The end of physicPhysicke is our body'sbodies health:
Why, Faustus, hast thou not attainedattain'd that end?
Are not thy billsbils hung upvp as monuments,
WherebyWherby whole citiesCities havehaue escapedescap't the plague
And thousand desperate maladies beenbeene curedcur'd?
Yet art thou still but Faustus, and a man.
Could'stCouldst thou make men to liveliue eternally,
Or being dead, raise them to life againagaine,
Then this profession were to be esteemedesteem'd.
PhysicPhysicke farewell.: Wherewhere is JustinianIustinian?

l.foreign: Si unavna eademque res legaturlegatus duobus,
AIter rem, alter valorem rei, etc&c.

sp.l: A petty case of paltry legaciesLegacies!,
ExhaereditareExhaereditari filium non potest pater, nisi--
Such is the subject of the institute,
And universalvniuersall body of the law.
This study fits a mercenaryMercenarie drudge,
Who aims at nothing but externalexternall trash,
Too servileseruile aad illiberalilliberall for memee.
When all is done, divinityDiuinitie is best;:
Jerome'sIeromes Bible, Faustus, view it well.:
Stipendium peccati, mors est.:" Haha!, Stipendiumstipendium, &c:.
The reward of sin is death? That'sthat's hard.:

l.foreign: Si peccasse, negamus, fallimur, et& nulla est in nobis veritas.:

sp.l: If we say that we havehaue no sinsinne
We deceivedeceiue ourselvesour selues, and there is no truth in usvs.
Why then belike we must sinsinne,
And so consequently die.,
AyI, we must die, an everlastingeuerlasting death.
What doctrine call you this:? Che sera, sera,:
What will be, shall be?; DivinityDiuinitie, adieuadeiw.
These metaphysicsMetaphisicks of magiciansMagitians,
And necromanticNegromantick booksbookes are heavenlyheauenly;,
Lines, circlesCircles, lettersLetters, charactersCharacters.
AyI, these are those that Faustus most desires.
O what a world of profitprofite and delight,
Of power, of honour, and omnipotence,
Is promised to the studiousStudious artisanArtizan?
All things that movemoue betweenbetweene the quiet polesPoles
Shall be at my command.: Emperors and Kings,
Are but obeyedobey'd in their severalseuerall provincesProuinces,:
But his dominion that exceeds in this,
Stretcheth as farfarre as doth the mind of man:
A sound magicianMagitian is a demi-godDemi-god.,
Here, tire my brainsbraines to get a Deity. Enter Wagner.
Wagner, commend me to my dearestdeerest friends,
The GermanGermane Valdes and Cornelius.,
Request them earnestly to visit me.

sp.p: I will sir. Exit.

sp.l: Their conference will be a greater helphelpe to me,
Then all my labours, plod I ne'erne're so fast.

div.stage: Enter the Good AngelAngell and Evil AngelSpirit.

sp.speaker: Good AngelA.
sp.l: O Faustus, lay that damned bookbooke aside,
And gaze not on it least it tempt thy soulsoule,
And heapheape God'sGods heavyheauy wrath uponvpon thy head.
ReadReade, readreade the scripturesScriptures: that is blasphemy.
Go forward, Faustus, in that famous artArt
Wherein all nature'snatures treasure is containedcontain'd.:
Be thou on earth as JoveIoue is in the skyskye,
Lord and Commander of these elements.:
How am I glutted with conceipt of this!?
Shall I make spirits fetch me what I please,?
ResolveResolue me of all ambiguities,?
PerformPerforme what desperate enterprise I will?
I'llI'le havehaue them flyflie to IndiaIndian for gold,;
RansackRansacke the oceanOcean for orientOrient pearlPearle,
And search all corners of the new-found worldnew-found-world
For pleasant fruitsfruites, and princelyPrincely delicates.
I'llI'le havehaue them read me strange philosophyPhilosophy,
And tell the secrets of all foreignforraine Kings.:
I'llI'le havehaue them wall all Germany with brassBrasse,
And make swift Rhine, circle faire WittenbergWittenberge.:
I'llI'le havehaue them fill the publicpublique schoolsSchooles with silkskill,
Wherewith the studentsStudents shall be bravelybrauely clad.
I'llI'le levyleauy soldierssouldiers with the coincoyne they bring,
And chase the Prince of Parma from our landLand,
And reignraigne sole kingKing of all the provincesProuinces.
Yea, stranger engines for the brunt of warwarre,
ThanThen was the fiery keelkeele at AntwerpAnwerpe's bridge,
I'llI'le make my servileseruile spirits to inventinuent.
Come, GermanGermane Valdes and Cornelius,
And make me blest with your sage conference. Enter Valdes.

Valdes, sweetsweete Valdes and Cornelius!,
and Cornelius.
Know that your words havehaue won me at the last.
To practice magicMagicke and concealed artsArts.
Philosophy is odious and obscure.:
Both lawLaw and physicPhysicke are for petty wits.,
'Tis magicmagick, magicmagick, that hath ravishedrauisht me.
Then gentle friends aid me in this attempt,
And I, that havehaue with subtle syllogismsSillogismes
GravelledGrauel'd the pastorsPastors of the GermanGermane Church,
And made the floweringflowring pride of Wittenberg
SwornSworne to my problemsProblemes, as th'infernalth'infernall spirits
On sweet Musaes when he came to hell,
Will be as cunning as Agrippa was,
Whose shadow made all Europe honour him.
Faustus, these booksbookes, thy wit, and our experience,
Shallshall make all nationsNations to canonizeCanonize usvs,
As Indian moorsMoores, obey their Spanish lordsLords.:
So shall the spirits of everyeuery element,
Be alwaysalwaies serviceableseruiceable to usvs three.:
Like lionsLyons shall they guard usvs when we please,
Like Almaine ruttersRutters with their horsemen'shorsemens stavesstaues,
Or LaplandLopland giantsGiants trotting by our sides.,
Sometimes like women or unweddedvowedded maidsMaides,:
Shadowing more beauty in their airyAirie browsbrowes,
ThanThen has the white breasts of the queenQueene of loveloue.
From Venice shall they drag huge argosiesArgosies,
And from America the goldenGolden fleeceFleece,
That yearlyyearely stuffedstuff'd old PhillipPhillips's treasury,
If learned Faustus will be resolute.
Valdes, as resolute am I in this,
As thou to liveliue, therefore object it not.
The miracles that magicmagick will performperforme,
Will make thee vow to study nothing else.
He that is grounded in Astrology,
EnrichedInricht with tongues, well seenseene in mineralsMinerals,
Hath all the principlesPrinciples magicMagick doth require.:
Then doubt not, Faustus, but to be renownedrenowm'd,
And more frequented for this mysterymysterie,
Then heretoforeheeretofore the Delphian oracleOracle.
The spirits tell me they can dry the sea,
And fetch the treasure of all foreignforraine wrackes,:
Yea, all the wealth that our fore-fathers hid,
Within the messy entrailsentrailes of the earth;:
Then tell me, Faustus, what shall we three want?
Nothing Cornelius.; O this cheerscheeres my soulsoule.:
Come, showshew me some demonstrations magicalMagicall,
That I may conjureconiure in some bushy groveGroue,
And havehaue these joysioies in full possession.
Then hast thee to some solitary groveGroue,
And bearbeare wise BaconBacons's, and Albanus' worksworkes,
The Hebrew Psalter, and Newnew Testament;
And whatsoeverwhatsoeuer else is requisite,
We will informinforme thee eree're our conference cease.
Valdes, first let him know the words of artArt,
And then all other ceremonies learnedlearn'd,
Faustus may try his cunning by himselfhimselfe.
First I'llI'le instruct thee in the rudiments,
And then wilt thou be perfecter then I.
Then come and dine with me, and after meatmeate
We'llWe'le canvasscanuase everyeuery quiddityquidditie thereof;:
For eree're I sleep, I'llI'le try what I can do:
This night I'llI'le conjureconiure thoughtho I die therefore. Exeunt.

sp.speaker: 1. ScholarSch.
sp.p:
I wonder what's become of Faustus that was wont
To make our schoolsschooles ring, with sic probo.Enter WagnerWag.

sp.speaker: 2. ScholarSch.
sp.p: That shall we presently know, here comes his boy.

sp.speaker: 1. ScholarSch.
sp.p: How now, sirrahsirra!, Where'swherets thy masterMaister?
God in heavenheauen knowsknowes.

sp.speaker: 2. ScholarSch.
sp.p: Why dost not thou know then?!
Yes, I know, but that followsfollowes not.

sp.speaker: 2. ScholarSch.
sp.p: Go to, sirrahsirra;, leaveleaue your jestingiesting, and& tell usvs where he is.

That followsfollowes not by force of argument, which
you, being licentiatesLicentiats, should stand uponvpon., Thereforetherefore, acknow-
ledge your errorerrour, and be attentiveattentiue.

sp.speaker: 2. ScholarSch.
sp.p: Then you will not tell usvs?

You are deceiveddeceiu'd, for I will tell you.: Yetyet if you
were not dunces, you would neverneuer askaske me such a question.:
For is he not Corpus naturale? Andand is not that mobileMobile? Then
wherefore should you askaske me such a question? But that I
am by nature phlegmaticflegmatique, slow to wrath, and& prone to lecheryletcherie
(to loveloue I would say) it were not for you to come within for-
tytie foot of the place of execution, although I do not doubt but
to see you both hangedhangd'd the next sessionsSessions. Thus, havinghauing tri-
umphedumpht overouer you, I will set my countenance like a precisionPrecisian,
and begin to speakspeake thus: trulyTruely my deardeere brethren, my masterMr.
is within at dinner, with Valdes and Cornelius, as this wine,
if it could speakspeake, would informinforme your worshipsWorships.: Andand so
the Lord blessblesse you, preservepreserue you, and keepkeepe you, my deardeere
brethren. Exit.

sp.speaker: 1. ScholarSch.
sp.l: O Faustus, then I fearfeare ityt which I havehaue long suspected:
That thou art fallenfalne into that damned artArt
For which they two are infamous through the world.

sp.speaker: 2. ScholarSch.
sp.l: Were he a stranger, not allayedallyed to me,
The danger of his soulsoule would make me mournmourne.:
But come, let usvs go, and informinforme the Rector.:
It may be his gravegraue counselcounsell may reclaimreclaime him.

sp.speaker: 1. ScholarSch.
sp.l: I fearfeare me, nothing will reclaimreclaime him now.

sp.speaker: 2. ScholarSch.
sp.l: Yet let usvs see what we can do. Exeunt.

sp.l: Now that the gloomy shadow of the night,
Longing to view Orion'sOrions drizzlingdrisling looklooke,
LeapsLeapes from th'Antarcticth'Antarticke world untovnto the skyskie,
And dimsdyms the welkinWelkin, with her pitchy breathe,:
Faustus, begin thine incantationsIncantations,
And try if devilsdeuils will obey thy hestHest,
Seeing thou hast prayedpray'd and sacrificedsacrific'd to them.
Within this circle is JehovahIehoua's's nameName,
Forward, and backward, anagrammatisedAnagramatis'd:
Th'abbreviatedTh'abreuiated names of holy saintsSaints,
Figures of everyeuery adjunctadiunct to the heavensheauens,
And charactersCharacters of signsSignes, and eveningeuening starsStarres,
By which the spirits are enforcedinforc'd to rise.:
Then fearfeare not, Faustus, to be resolute
And try the utmostvtmost magicMagicke can performperforme.

sp.l: I charge thee to returnreturne, and change thy shape.,
Thouthou art too uglyvgly to attend on me.:
Go and returnreturne an old Franciscan friarFrier;,
That holy shape becomes a devildeuill best. Exit Devildeuill.
I see there's virtuevertue in my heavenlyheauenly words.
Who would not be proficient in this artArt?
How pliant is this MephistophilisMephostophilis?
Full of obedience and humility,
Such is the force of magicMagicke, and my spellsspels.
Now, Faustus, what would'stwouldst thou havehaue me do?
I charge thee waitwaite uponvpon me whil'stwhilst I liveliue
To do what evereuer Faustus shall command.:
Be it to make the moonMoone drop from her sphereSphere,
Or the oceanOcean to overwhelmouerwhelme the world.
I am a servantseruant to great Lucifer,:
And may not follow thee without his leaveleaue.;
No more thanthen he commands, must we perform.
Did not he charge thee to appearappeare to me?
No, I came now hitherhether of mine oweowne accordaccdrd.
Did not my conjuringconiuring raise thee? Speakspeake.
That was the cause, but yet per accidensaccident;:
For when we hearheare one rackracke the name of God,
AbjureAbiure the scripturesScriptures, and his SaviorSauiour Christ,:
We flyflye in hope to get his glorious soulsoule;
Nor will we come, unlessvnlesse he usevse such meansmeanes,
Whereby he is in danger to be damneddamn'd.:
Therefore the shortest cut for conjuringconiuring
Is stoutly to abjureabiure all godlinessgodlinesse
And pray devoutlydeuoutely to the Prince of Hellhell.
So Faustus hath already done, and holds this principle:,
There is no chiefchiefe but onlyonely Beelzebub,:
To whom Faustus doth dedicate himselfhimselfe.
This word Damnation, terrifies not me,
For I confound hell in ElysiumElizium;:
My ghostGhost be with the old philosophersPhylosophers.
But leavingleauing these vainvaine trifles of men'smens soulssoules,
Tell me, what is that Lucifer, thy Lord?
Arch-regent and commanderCommander of all spiritsSpirits.
Was not that Lucifer an angelAngell once?
Yes, Faustus, and most dearlydeerely lovedlou'd of God.
How comes it then that he is Prince of DevilsDeuils?
O, by aspiring pride and insolence,
For which God threw him from the face of heavenheauen.
And what are you that liveliue with Lucifer?
UnhappyVnhappy spirits that liveliue with Lucifer,
ConspiredConspir'd against our God with Lucifer,
And are for evereuer damneddamn'd with Lucifer.
Where are you damneddamn'd?
In hell.
How comes it then that thou art out of hell?
Why this is hell,: nor am I out of it.
Think'st thou that I that saw the face of God,
And tasted the eternaleternall joysIoyes of heavenheauen
Am not tormented with ten thousand hellshels,
In being depriveddepriu'd of everlastingeuerlasting blissblisse?
O, Faustus, leaveleaue these frivolousfriuolous demandsdemandes,
Which strikestrikes a terror to my fainting soulsoule.
What, is great MephistophilisMephostophilis so passionate
For being depriveddepriued of the joysIoyes of heavenheauen?
LearnLearne thou of Faustus' manly fortitude,
And scornscorne those joysIoyes thou neverneuer shalt possesspossesse.
Go bearbeare these tidingstydings to great Lucifer,
Seeing Faustus hath incurredincur'd eternaleternall death,
By desperate thoughts against Jove'sIoues deityDeity.:
Say he surrenders upvp to him his soulsoule,
So he will spare him fourfoure and twenty yearsyeares,
Letting him liveliue in all voluptuousnessvoluptuousnesse,
HavingHauing thee evereuer to attend on me,
To givegiue me whatsoeverwhatsoeuer I shall askaske,;
To tell me whatsoeverwhatsoeuer I demand,:
To slay mine enemies, and to aid my friends,
And alwaysalwaies be obedient to my will.
Go, and returnreturne to mighty Lucifer,
And meet me in my studyStudy, at midnightMidnight,
And then resolveresolue me of thy master'sMaisters mind.
I will, Faustus. Exit.
Had I as many soulssoules, as there be starsStarres,
I'dI'de givegiue them all for MephistophilisMephostophilis.
By him, I'llI'le be great EmperorEmperour of the world,
And make a bridge, through the movingmouing airAire,
To passpasse the oceanOcean.: Withwith a band of men
I'llI'le joinioyne the hillsHils that bind the AfricAffrick shore,
And make that countryCountry, continent to SpainSpaine,
And both contributorycontributary to my crownCrowne.
The EmperorEmperour shall not liveliue, but by my leaveleaue,
Nor any Potentate of Germany.
Now that I havehaue obtainedobtain'd what I desireddesir'd
I'llI'le liveliue in speculation of this artArt
Till MephistophilisMephostophilis returnreturne againagaine.Exit.

sp.p: Come hither sirrahsirra boy.

Boy? O disgrace to my person.: Zounds! Boyboy in your
face!, Youyou have seenseene many boysboyes with beardswithbeards I am sure.
SirrahSirra, hast thou no comingscommings inIn?
Yes, and goings out too, you may see sir.

Alas poorpoore slaveslaue., Seesee how povertypouerty jestsiests in his naked-
nessnesse., I know the villain'sVillaines out of serviceseruice, and so hungry,
that I know he would givegiue his soulsoule to the devildeuill, for a shoul-
der of muttonMutton, thoughtho it were bloodbloud raw.

Not so neither; I had need to havehaue it well roastedrosted,
and good sauce to it, if I pay so deardeere, I can tell you.

SirrahSirra, wilt thou be my man and waitwaite on me? Andand
I will make thee go, like Qui mihi discipulus.
What, in verseVerse?
No, slaveslaue, in beaten silksilke, and stavesacrestaues-aker.

StavesacreStaues-aker? That'sthat's good to kill verminVermine.: Thenthen be-
like if I serveserue you, I shall be lousy.

Why, so thou shalt be, whether thou dost it or no.:
Forfor, sirrahsirra, if thou dost not presently bind thyselfthy toselfe meto forme for
sevenseuen yearsyeares, I'llI'le turnturne all the lice about thee into familiarsFamiliars,
and make them teartare thee in piecespeeces.

Nay, sir, you may savesaue yourselfyour aselfe laboura, forlabour, theyfor they
are as familiar with me, as if they paidpayd for their meatmeate and
drinkdrinke, I can tell you.
Well, sirrahsirra, leaveleaue your jestingiesting, and take these guildersGuilders.
Yes, marry, sir, and I thankthanke you tooto.

So, now thou art to bebee at an hour'showres warning,
whensoeverwhensoeuer, and wheresoeverwheresoeuer the devildeuill shall fetch thee.
Here, take your guildersGuilders; I'llI'le none of 'em.

Not I., Thouthou art pressedPrest., Prepareprepare thyselfthy, forselfe, Ifor willI will
presently raise upvp two devilsdeuils to carry thee away: Banio,
Belcher!.

Belcher? and Belcher come here., I'llI'le belch him.: I am
not afraid of a devildeuill. Enter two2 Devilsdeuils.
How now, sir, will you serveserue me now?
AyI, good Wagner, take away the devildeuill then.

Spirits, away!;
Nownow, sirrahsirra, follow me.

I will sir,; but harkhearke you masterMaister, you teach
me this conjuringconiuring occupationOccupation?

AyI, sirrahsirra, I'llI'le teach thee to turnturne thyselfthy toselfe ato doga, Dog,
or a catCat, or a mouseMouse, or a ratRat, or anythingany thing.

A dogDog, or a catCat, or a mouseMouse, or a ratRat?
O, bravebraue
Wagner.

VillainVillaine, call me masterMaister Wagner, and see that you
walkwalke attentivelyatttentiuely, and let your right eye be alwaysalwaies Dia-
metricallymetrally fixedfixt uponvpon my left heelheele, that thou may'stmaist, Quasi vesti-
gias nostras insistere.
Well, sir, I warrant you. Exeunt.

sp.l: Now, Faustus, must thou needs be damneddamn'd?
Can'stCanst thou not be savedsau'd?
What bootsbootes it then to thinkthinke on God or heavenHeauen?
Away with such vainvaine fancies, and despairdespaire,
DespairDespaire in GodGOD, and trust in BeelzebubBelzebub,
Now go not backward, Faustus;, be resolute.
Why waverswauerst thou? O something soundeth in mine eareare.
AbjureAbiure this magicMagicke, turnturne to God againagaine.
Why he lovesloues thee not.: The God thou serv'stseru'st is thine oweowne appetite
Wherein is fixedfixt the loveloue of BeelzebubBelzebub
To him, I'llI'le build an altarAltar and a churchChurch,
And offer lukewarmluke-warme bloodbloud, of new-bornnew borne babes.
Go forward, Faustus, in that famous artArt.

sp.speaker: Good AngelAn.
sp.l: SweetSweete Faustus, leaveleaue that execrable artArt.
Contrition, prayerPrayer, repentanceRepentance? Whatwhat of these?

sp.speaker: Good AngelA.
sp.l: O, they are meansmeanes to bring thee untovnto heavenheauen.
Rather, illusions, fruits of lunacy,.
That make them foolish that do usevse them most.

sp.speaker: Good AngelA.
sp.l: Sweet Faustus, think of heavenheauen, and& heavenlyheauenly things.
No, Faustus, thinkthinke of honour and of wealth.ExeuntEx. AngelsAn.
Wealth? Why the signorySignory of Embden shall be mine.:
When MephistophilisMephostophilis shall stand by me,
What power can hurt me? Faustus, thou art safe.
Cast no more doubts; MephistophilisMepho,:
And bring glad tidingstydings from great Lucifer
Is'tIst not midnight? Comecome, MephistophilisMephostophilis.
Now tell me, what saith Lucifer, thy Lord?.
That I shall waitwaite on Faustus whilst he livesliues,
So he will buy my serviceseruice with his soulsoule.
Already Faustus hath hazarded that for thee.
But now thou must bequeath it solemnly,
And writewright a deedDeed of giftGift with thine oweowne bloodbloud,;
For that security cravescraues Lucifer
If thou deny it I must backbacke to hell.
Stay, MephistophilisMephosto,. and tell me,
What good will my soulsoule do thy Lord?
Enlarge his KingdomKingdome.
Is that the reason why he tempts usvs thus?
Why, havehaue you any painpaine that torture other?
As great as havehaue the humane soulssoules of men.
But tell me Faustus, shall I havehaue thy soulsoule?
And I will be thy slaveslaue and waitwaite on thee,
And givegiue thee more then thou hast wit to askaske.
I MephistophilisMephostophilis, I'llI'le givegiue it him.
Then Faustus stab thy armArme courageouslycouragiously,
And bind thy soulsoule, that at some certaincertaine day
Great Lucifer may claimclaime it as his oweowne,
And then be thou as great as Lucifer

sp.p:
LoLoe MephistophilisMephosto: for loveloue of thee Faustus hath cut his armarme,
And with his proper bloodbloud assures his soulsoule to be great Lucifer'sLucifers,
sp.l: ChiefChiefe Lord and Regent of perpetualperpetuall night.
ViewVeiw here this bloodbloud that trickles from mine armarme,
And let it be propitious for my wish.
But, Faustus,
Write it in manner of a deedDeed of giftGift.
AyI, so I do,; but, MephistophilisMephostophilis ,
My bloodbloud congealscongeales, and I can write no more
I'lll'le fetch thee fire to dissolvedissolue it straightstreight. Exit.
What might the staying of my bloodbloud portend?
Is it unwillingvnwilling I should write this billbyll?
Why streamsstreames it not, that I may write afresha fresh?
Faustus givesgiues to thee his soulsoule: O there it stayedstaid.
Why should'stshouldst thou not? Isis not thy soulsoule thine oweowne?
Then write againagaine: Faustus givesgiues to thee his soulsoule.

div.stage: Enter MephistophilisMephostoph,: with the chaferChafer of fireFire.

sp.l: See, Faustus, here is fire;, set it on.
So, now the bloodbloud begins to clearcleere againagaine.:
Now will I make an end immediately.
What will not I do to obtainobtaine his soulsoule?
Consummatum est: this billbyll is ended,
And Faustus hath bequeathedbequeath'd his soulsoule to Lucifer
But what is this inscriptionInscription on mine armArme?
Homo fuge!, Whitherwhether should I flyflye?
If untovnto heavenheauen, he'llhee'le throw me downdowne to hell.
My sensessences are deceiveddeceiu'd;, here's nothing writ:
O yes, I see it plainplaine, eveneuen hereheere is writ
Homo fuge, yet shall not Faustus flyflye.
I'llI'le fetch him somewhat to delight his mindminde.

div.stage:
Enter DevilsDeuils, givinggiuing crownsCrownes and rich apparelapparell to
Faustus.: Theythey dance, and then depart.
Enter MephistophilisMephostophilis.

sp.l: What meansmeanes this showshew? Speakspeake, MephistophilisMephostophilis.
Nothing, Faustus, but to delight thy mind,
And let thee see what magicMagicke can performperforme
But may I raise such spirits when I please?
AyI, Faustus, and do greater things thanthen these.
Then, MephistophilisMephostophilis, receivereceiue this scrollscrole,
A deedDeed of giftGift, of body and of soulsoule.:
But yet conditionally, that thou performperforme
All covenantsCouenants, and articlesArticles, betweenbetweene usvs both.
Faustus, I swearsweare by hellHell and Lucifer
To effect all promises betweenbetweene usvs both.
Then hearheare me read it, MephistophilisMephostophilis,.
On these conditions following.

sp.p:
First, that Faustus may be a spirit in formforme and substance.
Secondly, that MephistophilisMephostophilis shall be his servantseruant, and be by
him commanded.
Thirdly, that MephistophilisMephostophilis shall dodoe for him, and bring him
whatsoeverwhatsoeuer.
Fourthly, that he shall be in his chamber or house invisibleinuisible.
Lastly, that hehee shall appearappeare to the said JohnIohn Faustus, at all
times, in what shape and formforme soeversoeuer he please.
I, JohnIohn Faustus of Wittenberg, Doctor, by these presents, dodoe
give both body and soulsoule to Lucifer, Prince of therhe East, and
his ministerMinister MephistophilisMephastophilis, and furthermore grant untovnto them
that fourfoure and twentytwentie yearsyeares being expired, and these articlesArticles
written being inviolate, full power to fetch or carry the
said JohnIohn Faustus' body and soulsoule, flesh, bloodbloud, into their ha-
bitation wheresoeverwheresoeuer.
By me JohnIohn Faustus.
SpeakSpeake, Faustus, do you deliverdeliuer this as your deedDeed?
Ay ,I take it, and the devildeuill givegiue thee good of it.

sp.l: So, now Faustus, askaske me what thou wilt.
First, I will question thee about hell:
Tell me, where is the place that men call hellHell?
UnderVnder the heavensheauens.
AyI, so are all things else,; but whereabouts?
Within the bowels of these elementsElements,
Where we are torturedtortur'd, and remainremaine foreverfor euer.
Hell hath no limits, nor is circumscribedcircumscrib'd,
In one selfselfe place,: but where we are is hell,
And where hell is there must we evereuer be.
And to be short, when all the world dissolvesdissolues,
And everyeuery creature shall be purifiedpurifi'd,
All places shall be hell that is not heavenheauen.
I thinkthinke hell'sHel's a fable.
AyI, thinkthinke so still, till experience change thy mind.
Why, dost thou think that Faustus shall be damneddamn'd?
AyI, of necessity, for here's the scrollscrowle
In which thou hast givengiuen thy soulsoule to Lucifer
AyI, and body too, but what of that?:
Think'st thou that Faustus, is so fond to imagine,
That after this life there is any painpaine?
No, these are trifles, and meremeere old wiveswiues talesTales.
But I am an instance to proveproue the contrary,:
For I tell thee I am damneddamn'd, and now in hell.
Nay, and this be hell, I'llI'le willingly be damneddamn'd.
What sleeping, eating, walking and disputing?
But leavingleauing this, let me havehaue a wife, the fairest maidMaid in
Germany, for I am wanton and lasciviouslasciuious, and cannot liveliue
without a wife.
Well, Faustus, thou shalt havehaue a wife.

div.stage: He fetches in a woman devildeuill.

sp.l: What sight is this?
Now, Faustus, wilt thou havehaue a wife?
Here's a hot whore indeed; no, I'llI'le no wife.
Marriage is but a ceremonialceremoniall toy,
And if thou lov'stlouest me thinkthinke no more of it.,
I'llI'le cull thee out the fairest courtesansCurtezans,
And bring them everyeuery morning to thy bed.:
She whom thine eye shall like, thy heart shall havehaue,
Were she as chaste as was Penelope,;
As wise as Saba, or as beautifulbeautifull
As was bright Lucifer before his fall.
Here, take this bookbooke, and peruse it well.:
The iterating ofo~f these lines brings gold;
The framing of this circle on the ground
Brings thunderThunder, whirl-windsWhirle-winds, stormStorme and lightningLightning.:
Pronounce this thrice devoutlydeuoutly to thyselfthy selfe,
And men in harnessharnesse shall appearappeare to thee,
Ready to execute what thou command'stcommandst.

ThanksThankes, MephistophilisMephostophilis,
for this sweetsweete bookbooke.

This will I keepkeepe as chary as my life.
Exeunt.
Learned Faustus
To know the secrets of Astronomy
GravenGrauen in the bookbooke of Jove'sIoues high firmament,
Did mount himselfhimselfe to scale Olympus' top.,
Being seated in a chariot burning bright,
DrawnDrawne by the strength of yokeyyoaky dragons'Dragons necks,
He now is gone to proveproue cosmographyCosmography,
And as I guessgesse will first arrivearriue at Rome,
To see the Pope and manner of his courtCourt,;
And take some part of holy PeterPeters's feast,
That to this day is highly solemnized.Exit Wagner.

div.stage: Enter Faustus in his studyStudy, and MephistophilisMephostophilis.

sp.l: When I behold the heavensheauens then I repent
And curse thee wicked MephistophilisMephostophilis,
Because thou hast depriveddepriu'd me of those joysIoyes.
'Twas thine oweowne seeking Faustus, thankthanke thyselfthy selfe.
But think'st thou heavenheauen is such a glorious thing?
I tell thee, Faustus, it is not halfhalfe so fairfaire
As thou, or any man that breathbreathe on earth.
How prov'stprou'st thou that?
'Twas made for man; then he's more excellent.
If heavenHeauen was made for man, 'twas made for me.:
I will renounce this magicMagicke and repent.

sp.speaker: Good AngelA.
sp.l: Faustus, repent yet God will pitypitty thee.
Thou art a spirit;, God cannot pity thee.
Who buzzeth in mine earseares I am a spirit?
Be I a devildeuill, yet God may pitypitty me.
Yea, God will pitypitty me if I repent.
AyI, but Faustus neverneuer shall repent.
My heart is hardenedhardned;, I cannot repent.:
Scarce can I name salvationsaluation, faith, or heavenheauen.
Swords, poisonpoyson, halters, and envenomedinuenomb'd steelsteele,
Are laid before me to dispatch my selfselfe,:
And long eree're this, I should havehaue done the deed,
Had not sweetsweete pleasure conqueredconquer'd deepdeepe despairdespaire.
HaveHaue not I made blind Homer sing to me
Of Alexander'sAlexanders loveloue, and OEnon'sOenons death?
And hath not he that built the wallswalles of Thebes,
With ravishingrauishing sound of his melodious harpHarpe,
Made musicmusicke with my MephistophilisMephostophilis?
Why should I die then, or basely despairdespaire?
I am resolvedresolu'd;, Faustus shall not repent.
Come, MephistophilisMephostophilis, let usvs dispute againagaine
And reason of divinedinine Astrology.
SpeakSpeake, are there many spheresSpheares aboveaboue the MoonMoone?
Are all celestialCelestiall bodies but one globeGlobe,
As is the substance of this centriccentricke earth?
As are the elements, such are the heavensheauens,
EvenEuen from the moonMoone untovnto the empirialEmperiall orbOrbe,
Mutually folded in each others spheresSpheares,
And jointlyiontly movemoue uponvpon one axle-treeAxle-tree,
Whose termine, is termedtearmed the world'sworlds wide polePole.
Nor are the names of SaturnSaturne, Mars, or JupiterIupiter,
FainedFain'd, but are eveningeuening starsStarres.
But havehaue they all one motion, both situ et& tempore?

sp.p:
All movemoue from eastEast to westWest in fourfoure and
twenty hourshoures, uponvpon the poles of the world, but differ in
their motions uponvpon the poles of the zodiacZodiacke.

sp.l: These slender questions Wagner can decide:
Hath MephistophilisMephostophilis no greater skill?
Who knowsknowes not the double motion of the planetsPlanets?
That the first is finishedfinisht in a naturalnaturall day;?
The second thus:, SaturnSaturne in 30 yearsyeares;
JupiterIupiter in 12, Mars in 4, the Sun, Venus, and
Mercury in a yearyeare; the moonMoone in twenty eight daysdaies.
sp.p:
These are freshmen'sfresh mens questions .: But tell me, hath everyeuery
SphereSpheare a dominionDominion, or intelligentiaIntelligentia?
AyI.
How many heavensHeauens, or spheresSpheares, are there?

Nine, the sevenseuen planetsPlanets, the firmamentFirmament, and the
empyrealEmperiall heavenheauen.
But is there not coelumCoelum igneum, et& cristallinumChristalinum?
No, Faustus, they be but fablesFables.

ResolveResolue me then in this one question:
Why are not conjunctionsConiunctions, oppositionsOppositions, aspectsAspects, eclipsesEclipses,
all at one time, but in some years we havehaue more, in some lesslesse?
Well, I am answeredanswer'd.: Nownow tell me, who made the world?
I will not.
Sweet MephistophilisMephostophilis, tell me.
MoveMoue me not, Faustus.
VillainVillaine, havehaue not I bound thee to tell me anythingany thing?

AyI, that is not against our kingdomKingdome.
This is.: Thou art damneddamn'd;, think thou of hell.
ThinkThinke, Faustus, uponvpon God ,that made the world.
Remember this this. Exit— Exit.

AyI, go, accursed spirit, to uglyvgly hell.:
'Tis thou hast damneddamn'd distressed Faustus' soulsoule. Is'tIst not too late?
Too late.

sp.speaker: Good .Angel
sp.p: NeverNeuer too late, if Faustus will repent.
If thou repent, devilsdeuils will tearteare thee in piecespeeces.

sp.speaker: Good .Angel
sp.p: Repent and they shall neverneuer raise thy skin. ExitEx. AngelsA.

O, Christ my SaviorSauiour, my SaviorSauiour,
HelpHelpe to savesaue distressed Faustus' soulsoule.

sp.l: Christ cannot savesaue thy soulsoule, for he is justiust.,
There's none but I havehaue interest in the same.

sp.p: O, what art thou that look'st so terribly?.
I am Lucifer, and this is my companion Prince in hell.
O, Faustus, they are come to fetch thy soulsoule.
We are come to tell thee thou dost injureiniure usvs.
Thou call'stcalst on Christ contrary to thy promise.
Thou should'st not thinkthinke on God.
ThinkThinke on the devildeuill.
And his dam tooto.

sp.l: Nor will Faustus henceforth.: Pardonpardon him for this;,
And Faustus vowsvowes neverneuer to looklooke to heavenheauen.
So shalt thou show thy selfselfe an obedient servantseruant,
And we will highly gratify thee for it.

sp.p:
Faustus, we are come from hell in person to showshew
thee some pastime.: Sitsit downdowne and thou shalt behold the sevenseuen
deadly sinssinnes appearappeare to thee in their ownowne proper shapes
and likenesslikenesse.

That sight will be as pleasant to me, as Paradise
was to Adam the first day of his creation.

TalkTalke not of ParadiseParadice or creationCreation, but markmarke
the showshew., Gogo, MephistophilisMephostoph,. fetch them in.

Now, Faustus, question them of their names and
dispositions.
That shall I soonsoone.: What art thou the first?

sp.speaker: Pride.
sp.p:
I am Pride; I disdaindisdaine to havehaue any parents.: I am
like to OvidOuids's Flea;, I can creepcreepe intoi everynto cornereuery ofcorner aof a
wenchWench.: Sometimes, like a periwigPerriwig, I sit uponvpon her
browBrow.: Nextnext, like a necklaceNeckelace, I hang about her neckNecke.:
Then, like a fanFan of feathersFeathers, I kisskisse her,; andAnd then tur-
ning myselfmy toselfe ato wroughta smockwrought doSmocke whatdo Iwhat listI. Butlist. fieBut, fye,
what a smell is hereheere? I'llI'le not speakspeake a word more for a
king'sKings ransomransome, unlessvnlesse the ground be perfumedperfum'd, and coveredcouer'd
with cloth of arrasArras.

Thou art a proud knaveknaue indeed.: What art thou
second?

I am CovetousnessCouetousnesse,: begotten of an old churlChurle
in a leather bag,; and might I now obtainobtaine my wish, this house
you and all, should turnturne to Gold, that I might locklocke you safe
into my chestChest.: O my sweetsweete Gold!
And what art thou the third?

I am EnvyEnuy, begotten of a chimney-sweeperChimney-sweeper, and
an oyster-wifeOyster-wife.: I cannot read, and therefore wish all books
burnedburn'd. I am leanleane with seeing others eateate.: O that there
would come a famine overouer all the world, that all might die, and
I liveliue alone, then thou should'st see how fat I'dI'de be. But must
thou sit, and I stand? Comecome downdowne with a vengeance.
Out enviousenuious wretch.: But what art thou the fourth?

sp.speaker: Wrath.
sp.p:
I am Wrath.: I had neither father nor mother;, I
leapt out of a lion'sLyons mouth when I was scarce an hourhoure old,
and evereuer since havehaue runrunne upvp and downdowne the world with these
case of rapiersRapiers, wounding myselfmy whenselfe Iwhen couldI getcould noneget none
to fight withalwithall.: I was bornborne in hell, and look to it, for some
of you shall be my father.
And what art thou the fifthfift?

I am Gluttony; my parents are all dead, and the de-
viluill a pennypeny they havehaue left me, but a small pensionpention, and that
buysbuyes me thirty mealsmeales a day, and ten beaversBeauers: a small trifle
to suffice nature. I come of a royalRoyall pedigreePedigree, my father
was a gammonGammon of baconBacon, and my mother was a Hogs-
head of claretClaret wineWine. My godfathers were these: Peter-
Pickled-herringpickeld-herring, and Martin Martlemasse-beefMartlemasse-beefe: But my god-
mother, O she was an ancient gentlewomanGentlewoman., Herher name was
Margery March-beerMarch-beere.: Now, Faustus, thou hast heard all my
progeny;, wilt thou bid me to supper?
Not I.
Then the devildeuill chokechooke thee.
Choke thyselfthy selfe gluttonGlutton.: What art thou the sixthsixt?

sp.speaker: Sloth.
sp.p:
Hey ho,; I am Sloth.: I was begotten on a sunny-
bank.: Heyhey ho,: I'llI'le not speak a word more for a king'skings ransomransome.
And what are you MistressMistris Minkes, the seventhseuenth and& last?

Who, I, sirI sir? I am one that lovesloues an inch of raw
muttonMutton, better thanthen an ell of friedfryde stockfishStockfish,: and the first
letter of my name begins with LecheryLetchery.
Away to hell!, Awayaway, on, piper!. ExitEx. the Seven7 Deadlysinssinnes.
O, how this sight doth delight my soulsoule.
But, Faustus, in hell is all manner of delight.

O, might I see hell, and returnreturne againagaine safe., Howhow
happy were I then.

ThanksThankes mighty Lucifer.:
This will I keepkeepe as chary as my life.
Now, Faustus, farewell.

Farewell, great Lucifer.: Comecome, MephistophilisMephostophilis
Exeunt omnes, severalseuerall wayswaies.

What, Dick, looklooke to the horses there till I come againagaine.
I havehaue gotten one of Doctor Faustus's conjuringconiuring booksbookes, and
now we'llwe'le havehaue such knaveryknauery, as't passes.

sp.speaker: Dick.
sp.p: What, Robin, you must come away and& walk the horses.

I walkwalke the horses, I scornedscorn't 'faith, I havehaue other
matters in hand;, let the horses walk themselvesthemselues and they will.
A per seperse a, t. h. e the: o per se o deny orgon, gorgon.: Keepkeepe
further from me, O thou illiterate, and unlearnedvnlearned hostlerHostler.

sp.speaker: Dick.
sp.p:
'Snails'Snayles, what hast thou got there, a book? Whywhy thou
can'stcanst not tell ne'erne're a word on't.

That thou shalt see presently.: Keepkeep out of the cir-
cle, I say, lestleast I send you into the ostryOstry with a vengeance.

sp.speaker: Dick.
sp.p:
That's like 'faith.: Youyou had best leaveleaue your foolery,
for, an my masterMaister come, he'llhe'le conjureconiure you 'faith.

My masterMaister conjureconiure me? I'llI'le tell thee what, an my
masterMaister come here, I'llI'le clap as fairfaire a pairpaire of hornshornes on's
head as e'ere're thou saw'stsawest in thy life.

sp.speaker: Dick.
sp.p: Thou need'stneedst not do that, for my mistressMistresse hath done it.

Ay I, there be of usvs here, that havehaue waded as deepdeepe in-
to matters, as other men, if they were disposed to talktalke.

sp.speaker: Dick.
sp.p:
A plague take you!, I thought you did not sneaksneake
upvp and downdowne after her for nothing. But I pritheeprethee tell me, in
good sadnesssadnesse Robin, is that a conjuringconiuring bookbooke?

Do but speakspeake what thou'dthou's havehaue me to do, and I'llI'le
do't.: If thou'dthou't dance naked, put off thy clothescloathes, and I'llI'le
conjureconiure thee about presently.: Or if thou'dthou's go but to the ta-Ta-
vernuerne with me, I'llI'le givegiue thee white wine, red wine, claret
wine, sackSacke, muskadineMuska, malmeseydine, andMalmesey whippincrustand. Whippincrust,
Holdhold belly hold, and we'llwee'le not pay one pennypeny for it.

sp.speaker: Dick.
sp.p:
O bravebraue, pritheeprethee let's to it presently, for I am as
dry as a dog.

Come, then, let's away.
Exeunt.

div.l: Learned Faustus to find the secrets of Astronomy,
GravenGrauen in the bookbooke of Jove'sIoues high firmament,
Did mount him upvp to scale OlympusOlimpus' top,.
Where sitting in a chariotChariot burning bright,
DrawnDrawne by the strength of yoked dragons'Dragons necksneckes;
He viewsviewes the cloudscloudes, the planetsPlanets, and the starsStarres,
The tropicTropick, zonesZones, and quarters of the skyskye,
From the bright circle of the horned moonMoone,
EvenEuen to the height of Primum Mobile:.
And whirling round with this circumference,
Within the concaveconcaue compasscompasse of the polePole,
From eastEast to westWest his dragonsDragons swiftly glide,
And in eight daysdaies did bring him home againagaine.
Not long he stayed within his quiet house,
To rest his bones after his weary toiltoyle,
But new exploits do hale him out againagen
And mounted then uponvpon a dragon'sDragons backbacke,
That with his wings did part the subtle airaire.:
He now is gone to proveproue Cosmography,
That measures costs, and kingdomskingdomes of the earth.:
And as I guessguesse will first arrivearriue at Rome,
To see the Pope and manner of his courtCourt,
And take some part of holy PeterPeters's feast,
The which this day is highly solemnized. Exit.

sp.l: HavingHauing now my good MephistophilisMephostophilis,
PassedPast with delight the stately townTowne of Trier,:
EnvironedInuironed round with airy mountainmountaine tops,
With wallswals of flintFlint, and deepdeepe entrenchedintrenched lakesLakes,
Not to be wonwonne by any conquering princePrince.
From Paris next, costing the realmRealme of France,
We saw the riverRiuer MainMaine, fall into RhineRhines,
Whose banksbankes are set with grovesGroues of fruitfulfruitfull vinesVines.
Then upvp to Naples, rich Campania,
Whose buildings fairfaire, and gorgeous to the eye,
The streetsstreetes straight forth, and paledpaued with finest brickbricke.
There saw we learned MaroMaroes's golden tombtombe,:
The way he cut an English mile in length,
Through a rockrocke of stone in one night'snights space.:
From thence to Venice, Padua, and the eastEast,
In one of which a sumptuous templeTemple stands,
That threatsthreates the starsstarres with her aspiring top,
Whose frame is pavedpaued with sundry coloured stones,
And roofedroof't aloft with curious workworke in gold.
Thus hitherto hath Faustus spent his time.
But tell me now, what resting place is this?
Hast thou, as erstearst I did command,
Conducted me within the wallswalles of Rome?
I havehaue my Faustus, and for proofproofe thereof,
This is the goodly palacePalace of the Pope,:
And cause we are no common guests,
I choosechuse his privypriuy chamber for our usevse.
I hope his HolinessHolinesse will bid usvs welcome.
All's one, for we'llwee'l be bold with his venisonVenson.
But now, my Faustus, that thou may'stmaist perceiveperceiue,
What Rome containscontaines for to delight thine eyes.
Know that this cityCity stands uponvpon sevenseuen hillshils,
That underpropvnderprop the ground-workground-worke of the same.:
JustIust through the midst runsrunnes flowing TiberTybers's streamstreame,
With winding banksbankes that cut it in two parts;
OverOuer the which two stately Bridges leanleane,
That make safe passage, to each part of Rome.
UponVpon the Bridge, calledcall'd Ponto Angelo,
Erected is a castleCastle passing strong,
Where thou shalt see such store of ordinanceOrdinance,
As that the double cannonsCannons forgedforg'd of brassbrasse,
Do watch the number of the daysdaies containedcontain'd,
Within the compasscompasse of one completecompleat yearyeare.:
Beside the gates, and high pyramidsPyramydes,
That JuliusIulius Caesar brought from AfricaAffrica.
Now by the kingdomsKingdomes of infernalInfernall ruleRule,
Of StyxStix, of Acheron, and the fiery lakeLake,
Of ever-burningeuer-burning PhlegethonPhlegeton, I swearsweare,
That I do long to see the monumentsMonuments
And situation of bright splendent Rome.,
Come, therefore, let's away
Nay stay, my Faustus;: I know you'd see the Pope
And take some part of holy PeterPeters's feast,
The which this day with high solemnity,
This day is held through Rome and Italy,
In honour of the Pope'sPopes triumphant victory.
SweetSweete MephistophilisMephosto,. thou pleasest me.
Whilst I am here on earth,: letLet me be cloyedcloyd
With all things that delight the heart of man.
My fourfoure and twenty yearsyeares of liberty
I'llI'le spend in pleasure and in dalliancedaliance,
That Faustus' name, whilst this bright frame doth stand,
May be admired through the furthest landLand.
'Tis well said, Faustus., Comecome, then, stand by me
And thou shalt see them come immediately.
Nay, stay my gentle MephistophilisMephostophilis,
And grant me my request, and then I go.
Thou know'st within the compasscompasse of eight daysdaies,
We viewedveiw'd the face of heavenheauen, of earth and hell.
So high our dragonsDragons soaredsoar'd into the airaire,
That looking downdowne the earth appearedappear'd to me,
No bigger thanthen my hand in quantity.
There did we view the kingdomsKingdomes of the world,
And what might please mine eye, I there beheld.
Then in this showshew let me an actorActor be,
That this proud Pope may Faustus' cunningcomming see.
Let it be so, my Faustus, but first stay,
And view their triumphs, as they passpasse this way.
And then devisedeuise what best contents thy mindminde,
By comingcomming in thine artArt to crosscrosse the Pope,
Or dash the pride of this solemnity,;
To make his monksMonkes and abbotsAbbots stand like apesApes,
And point like antiquesAntiques at his triple crownCrowne:
To beatbeate the beadsbeades about the friars'Friers patesPates,
Or clap huge hornshornes, uponvpon the cardinals'Cardinals heads,:
Or any villainyvillany thou can'stcanst devisedeuise,
And I'llI'le performperforme it, Faustus.: Harkheark, they come:
This day shall make thee be admiredadmir'd in Rome.

div.stage:
Enter the Cardinals and Bishops, some bearing crosiersCrosiers, some
the pillarsPillars, MonksMonkes and FriarsFriers, singing their processionProcession.:
Then the Pope, and Raymond, King of Hunga-
ry, with Bruno led in chaines.

sp.speaker: Pope.
sp.p: Cast downdowne our footstoolFoot-stoole.

sp.l: Saxon Bruno stoopstoope,
Whilst on thy backbacke his Holinesshollinesse ascends
Saint PeterPeters's chairChaire and stateState pontificalPontificall.
Proud Lucifer, that stateState belongs to me.:
But thus I fall to Peter, not to thee.

sp.speaker: Pope.
sp.l: To me and Peter, shalt thou grovelinggroueling lie,
And crouch before the papalPapall dignity.:
Sound trumpetsTrumpets then, for thus SaintPeterPeters's heirHeire,
From Bruno's backbacke, ascends Saint Peter's chairChaire.
Thus, as the godsGods, creepcreepe on with feetfeete of wool,
Long ere with ironIron hands they punish men,
So shall our sleeping vengeance now arise,
And smite with death thy hated enterprise.
Lord cardinalsCardinals of France and Padua,
Go forthwithforth-with to our holy consistoryConsistory,
And read amongst the statutesStatutes decretalDecretall,
What by the holy councilCouncell held at Trent,
The sacred synodSinod hath decreed for him,
Thatlthat dothcloth assume the papalPapall governmentgouernment,
Without election, and a true consent.:
Away, and bring usvs word with speed.

sp.speaker: 1 CardinalCard.
sp.l: We go, my Lord. Exeunt Cardinals.

sp.speaker: Pope.
sp.l: Lord Raymond.
Go, hast thee, gentle MephistophilisMephostophilis,
Follow the cardinalsCardinals to the consistoryConsistory,;
And as they turnturne their superstitious booksBookes,
strike them with sloth, and drowsy idlenessidlenesse,;
And make them sleepsleepe so sound, that in their shapes,
ThyselfThy selfe and I , may parly with this Pope,:
This proud confronter of the EmperorEmperour,
And in despite of all his HolinessHolinesse
Restore this Bruno to his liberty,
And bearbeare him to the statesStates of Germany.

sp.p: Faustus, I gogoe.

sp.l: DispatchDispath it soonsoone.,
The Pope shall curse that Faustus came to Rome.

sp.speaker: Bruno.
sp.l: Pope Adrian, let me havehaue some right of lawLaw;,
I was elected by the EmperorEmperour.

sp.speaker: Pope.
sp.l: We will depose the EmperorEmperour for that deed,
And curse the people that submit to him;
Both he and thou shalt stand excommunicate,
And interdict from churchesChurches privilegepriuiledge,
And all society of holy men.:
He growsgrowes tooto proudprowd in his authority,
Lifting his loftyloftie headh ead aboveaboue the clouds,
And like a steepleSteeple overpeersouer-peeres the churchChurch.
But we'llwee'le pullpul downdowne his haughty insolence,:
And as Pope Alexander, our progenitorProgenitour,
TrodTrode on the neck of GermanGermane FrederickFredericke,
Adding this golden sentence to our praise,;
That PeterPeters's heirsheires should tread on emperorsEmperours,
And walkwalke uponvpon the dreadfuldreadfull adder'sAdders backbacke,
Treading the lionLyon, and the dragonDragon downdowne.
And fearlessfearelesse spurnspurne the killing basiliskBasiliske,:
So will we quell that haughty schismaticSchismatique,;
And by authority apostolicApostolicall
Depose him from his regalRegall governmentGouernment.
Pope JuliusIulius swore to princelyPrincely Sigismond,
For him, and the succeeding Popes of Rome,
To hold the emperorsEmperours their lawfullawfull lordsLords.

sp.speaker: Pope.
sp.l: Pope JuliusIulius did abuse the Church'sChurches ritesRites,
And therefore none of his decreesDecrees can stand.
Is not all power on earth bestowed on usvs?
And therefore thoughtho we would we cannot e'ererre.
Behold this silverSiluer beltBelt whereto is fixedfixt
SevenSeuen golden seales fast sealed with sevenseuen sealsseales,
In token of our seven-foldseuen-fold power from heavenheauen,
To bindbinde or loose, lock fast, condemncondemne, or judgeiudge,
ResignResigne, or seale, or what so pleaseth usvs.
Then he and thou, and all the world shall stoopstoope,
Or be assured of our dreadfuldreadfull curse,
To light as heavyheauy as the painspaines of hell.
sp.stage: Enter Faustus and MephistophilisMephosto,. like the Cardinals.

sp.l: Now tell me, Faustus, are we not fitted well?
Yes, MephistophilisMephosto,. and two such cardinalsCardinals
Ne'erNe're servedseru'd a holy Pope, as we shall do.
But whil'stwhilst they sleepsleepe within the consistoryConsistory,
Let usvs salute his reverendreuerend FatherhoodFather-hood.
Behold, my Lord, the cardinalsCardinals are returnedreturn'd.

sp.speaker: Pope.
sp.l: Welcome, gravegraue Fathers, answeranswere presently,
What havehaue our holy councilCouncell there decreed,
Concerning Bruno and the EmperorEmperour,
In quittance of their late conspiracyconspiracie
Against our stateState, and papalPapall dignitydignitie?
Most sacred patronPatron of the Church of Rome,
By full consent of all the synodSynod
Of priestsPriests and prelatesPrelates, it is thus decreed:
That Bruno, and the GermanGermane EmperorEmperour
Be held as Lollords, and bold schismaticsSchismatiques,
And proud disturbers of the Church'sChurches peace.
And if that Bruno by his ownowne assent,
Without enforcementinforcement of the German peersPeeres,
Did seekseeke to wearweare the triple diademDyadem,
And by your death to climbclime SaintS. PeterPeters's chairChaire,.,
The statutesStatutes decretalDecretall havehaue thus decreed:,
He shall be straightstreight condemnedcondemn'd of heresyheresie,
And on a pile of fagotsFagots burnt to death.

sp.speaker: Pope.
sp.l: It is enough.: Herehere, take him to your charge,
And bearbeare him straightstreight to Ponto Angelo,
And in the strongest towerTower encloseinclose him fast.,
TomorrowTo morrow, sitting in our consistoryConsistory,
With all our collegeColledge of gravegraue cardinalsCardinals,
We will determine of his life or death.
Here, take his triple crownCrowne along with you,
And leaveleaue it in the Church'sChurches treasury.
Make haste againagaine, my good lordLord cardinalsCardinalls,
And take our blessing apostolicApostolicall.
So, so, was neverneuer devilDiuell thus blessedblest before.
Away, sweet MephistophilisMephosto, be gone.,
The cardinalsCardinals will be plaguedplagu'd for this anon. ExitEx. FaustusFa .&andamp; MephistophilisMep.

sp.speaker: Pope.
sp.l: Go presently, and bring a banquetbanket forth,
That we may solemnize SaintS. PeterPeters's feast,
And with Lord Raymond, King of Hungary,
DrinkDrinke to our late and happy victory. Exeunt.

div.stage:
A sennetSenit while the banquetBanquet is brought in,; and then enterEnter
Faustus and MephistophilisMephastophilis in their ownowne
shapes.

sp.l: Now, Faustus, come prepare thyselfthy selfe for mirth;,
The sleepy cardinalsCardinals are hard at hand,
To censure Bruno, that is posted hence,
And on a proud pacedpac'd steedSteed, as swift as thought,
Flies o'erore the AlpsAlpes to fruitfulfruitfull Germany,
There to salute the woefulwofull EmperorEmperour.
The Pope will curse them for their sloth todayto day.
That slept both Bruno and his crowncrowne away.,
But now, that Faustus may delight his mindminde,
And by their folly make some merriment,
Sweet MephistophilisMephasto,: so charmcharme me here,
That I may walkwalke invisibleinuisible to all,
And dodoe what e'erere I please, unseenvnseene of any.
Faustus, thou shalt., Thenthen kneelkneele downdowne presently.,

lg.l: Whil'stWhilst on thy head I lay my hand,
And charmcharme thee with this magicMagicke wand,
First wearweare this girdle, then appearappeare
The planetsPlanets sevenseuen, the gloomy airaire,
Hell and the Furies'Furies forked hairhaire,
Pluto's blueblew fire, and Hecat's tree,
With magicMagicke spellsspels so compasscompasse thee,
That no eye may thy body see.

sp.l: So , Faustus, now for all their holinessholinesse,
Do what thou wilt;, thou shalt not be discerneddiscern'd.
ThanksThankes, MephistophilisMephosto.: Nownow, FriarsFriers, take heed,
Lest Faustus make your shavenshauen crownscrownes to bleed.
Faustus, no more.: Seesee where the cardinalsCardinals come.
sp.stage:
Enter Pope and all the Lords. Enter the Cardinals
with a bookBooke.

sp.speaker: Pope.
sp.l: Welcome, lordLord cardinalsCardinals.: Comecome sit downdowne.
Lord Raymond, take your seatseate;, FriarsFriers attend,
And see that all things be in readinessreadinesse,

sp.speaker: 1. CardinalCard.
sp.l: First, may it please your sacred HolinessHolinesse,
To view the sentence of the reverendreuerend synodSynod,
Concerning Bruno and the EmperorEmperour.

sp.speaker: Pope.
sp.l: What needs this question? Did I not tell you,
TomorrowTo morrow we would sit i'th'consistoryi'th Consistory,
And there determine of his punishment?
You brought usvs word eveneuen now, it was decreed,
That Bruno and the cursed EmperorEmperour
Were by the holy councilCouncell both condemnedcondemn'd
For loathedlothed Lollords, and base schismaticsSchismatiques.:
Then wherefore would you havehaue me view that bookbooke?

sp.speaker: 1. CardinalCard.
sp.l: Your Grace mistakes;, you gavegaue usvs no such charge.
Deny it not;, we all are witnesses
That Bruno here was late delivereddeliuered you,
With his rich triple crowncrowne to be reservedreseru'd,
And put into the Church'sChurches treasury.
By holy Paul we saw them not.

sp.speaker: Pope.
sp.l: By Peter, you shall diedye,
UnlessVnlesse you bring them forth immediatelyimmediatly.:
Hale them to prison, lade their limbslimbes with gyvesgyues.:
False prelatesPrelates, for this hatefulhatefull treachery,
CursedCurst be your soulssoules to hellish misery.
So, they are safe.: Nownow, Faustus, to the feast,
The Pope had neverneuer such a frolicfrolicke guest.

sp.speaker: Pope.
sp.l: Lord Archbishop of ReamsReames, sit downdowne with usvs.
I thankthanke your HolinessHolinesse.
Fall to, the DevilDiuell choke you an you spare.

sp.speaker: Pope.
sp.l: Who's that spoke? FriarsFriers, looklooke about.,
Lord Raymond, pray fall totoo;, I am beholding
to the Bishop of MilanMillaine, for this so rare a present.
I thankthanke you sir. Snatches it

sp.speaker: Pope.
sp.l: How now!? Whowho snatchedsnatch't the meat from me?!
VillainsVillaines, why speakspeake you not?
My good Lord Archbishop, here'sheres a most daintydaintie dish,
Was sent me from a cardinalCardinall in France.
I'llI'le havehaue that too.

sp.speaker: Pope.
sp.l: What Lollards do attend our HolinessHollinesse,
That we receivereceiue such great indignity? Fetchfetch me some wine.
AyI, pray do, for Faustus is a dry.

sp.speaker: Pope.
sp.l: Lord Raymond, I drink untovnto your grace.
I pledge your grace.

sp.speaker: Pope.
sp.l: My wine gone too? Yeyee Lubbers look about
And find the man that doth this villainyvillany,
Or by our sanctitude you all shall die.
I pray my lordsLords havehaue patience at this
Troublesome banquet.

sp.speaker: BishopBish.
sp.p:
Please it your holinessholinesse, I thinkthinke it be some ghostGhost
crept out of Purgatory, and now is come untovnto your Holi-holi-
nessnesse for his pardon.

sp.speaker: Pope.
sp.l: It may be so.:
Go then command our priestsPriests to sing a dirgeDirge,
To lay the fury of this same troublesome ghost.
How now? Mustmust everyeuery bit be spiced with a crossCrosse?
Nay, then, take that.

sp.speaker: Pope.
sp.l: O, I am slainslaine;, help me my lordsLords.:
O, come and help to bearbeare my body hence.:
DamnedDamb'd be this soulsoule foreverfor euer, for this deed.

div.stage: Exeunt the Pope and his traintraine.

sp.speaker: Me.
sp.p:
Now, Faustus, what will you do now,? for I can tell you
you'llYou'le be cursedcurst with bellBell, bookBooke, and candleCandle?.

sp.l: Bell, bookBooke, and candleCandle:; candleCandle, bookBooke, and bellBell;,
Forward and backward, to curse Faustus to hell.

sp.p:
Come, brethren, let's about our businessbusinesse with
good devotiondeuotion.
Cursed be he that stole his Holiness'holinesse meatmeate from the tableTable.
Maledicat Dominus.
Cursed be he that struckstroke his Holinessholinesse a blow the face.
Maledicat Dominus.
Cursed be he that struckstrucke Friarfryer Sandelo a blow on the pate.
Maledicat DominusDom.

Maledicat DominusDom.

sp.speaker: Dick.
sp.p:
SirrahSirra Robin, we were best looklooke that your devildeuill

boy followsfollowes usvs at the hard heelsheeles.

'Tis no matter;, let him come.; Ifan he follow usvs, I'llI'le so

him.: Letlet me see the cup.

sp.speaker: Dick.
sp.p:
Here 'tis.: Yonder he comes.: Now Robin, now or
neverneuer, showshew thy cunning.

O, are you here? I am glad I havehaue found you;, you
are a couple of fine companions.: Praypray where's the cup you
stole from the tavernTauerne?

How, how? Wewe stealsteale a cup? Taketake heed what you say;,
we looklooke not like cup-stealers I can tell you.

NeverNeuer deny't, for I know you havehaue it, and I'llI'le
search you.

Search me? AyI and spare not.: Holdhold the cup Dick.,
Comecome, come, search me, search me.
Come on sirrahsirra, let me search you now.

sp.speaker: Dick.
sp.p:
AyI, ayI, do, do;, hold the cup Robin., Ifearfeare not your
searching; we scornscorne to stealsteale your cups I can tell you.

NeverNeuer outfaceout meface forme thefor matterthe, formatter, surefor thesure cupthe iscup is
betweenbetweene you two.
Nay, there you lie;, 'tis beyond usvs both.

sp.speaker: VintnerVint.
sp.p:
A plague take you;, I thought 'twas your knaveryknauery
to take it away.: Come, givegiue it me againagaine.

AyI, much., Whenwhen, can you tell?: Dick, make me a cir-
cle, and stand close at my backbacke, and stir not for thy life., Vint-
ner, you shall havehaue your cup anon;, say nothing, Dick.: O per
se o, demogorgon., Belcher and MephistophilisMephostophilis.

sp.l: You princelyPrincely legionsLegions of infernalinfernall ruleRule,
How am I vexed by these villainsvillaines charmsCharmes?
From Constantinople havehaue they brought me now,
OnlyOnely for pleasure of these damned slavesslaues.

sp.p:
By ladyLady sir, you havehaue had a shroud journeyiourney of it;,
Willwill it please you to take a shoulder of muttonMutton to supper, and
a testerTester in your purse, and go backbacke againagaine?.

sp.speaker: Dick
sp.p:
AyI, I pray you heartily sir,; for wewee calledcal'd you but in
jestieast, I promise you.

sp.l: To purge the rashnessrashnesse of this cursed deed,
First, be thou turned to this uglyvgly shape,
For apishApish deeds transformed to an apeApe.

sp.p:
O bravebraue, an apeApe? I pray sir, let me havehaue the carry-
ing of him about to showshew some trickstrickes.

And so thou shalt: be thou transformedtransform'd to a dog, and
carry him uponvpon thy backbacke.; Awayaway, be gone.

A dog? That'sthat's excellent.: Letlet the maidsMaids looklooke well to
their porridge-pots, for I'llI'le into the kitchenKitchin presently.: Comecome,
Dick, come. Exeunt the two ClownsClownes.

sp.l: Now with the flames of ever-burningeuer-burning fire,
I'llI'le wing myselfmy selfe and forth-with flyflie amainamaine

UntoVnto my Faustus to the great Turk'sTurkes courtCourt.
Exit.

div.stage: Enter Martino, and Frederick at severalseuerall doorsdores.

sp.l: What ho, officersOfficers, gentlemenGentlemen!,
HieHye to the presence to attend the EmperorEmperour,
Good FrederickFredericke, see the roomsroomes be voidedvoyded straight;,
His majestyMaiesty is comingcomming to the hallHall.;
Go backbacke, and see the stateState in readinessreadinesse.
But where is Bruno, our elected popePope,
That on a fury'sfuries back came post from Rome.,
Will not his grace consort the EmperorEmperour?.
O yes, and with him comes the GermanGermane conjurorConiurer,.
The learned Faustus, fame of WittenbergWittenberge,
The wonder of the world for magicMagick artArt,;
And he intends to showshew great Carolus,
The race of all his stout progenitors,;
And bring in presence of his majestyMaiesty,
The royalroyall shapes and warlike semblances
Of Alexander and his beauteous paramourParamour.
Where is BenvolioBenuolio?
Fast asleepa sleep I warrant you.,
He took his rouse with stoupsstopes of Rhennish wine,
So kindly yesternight to BrunoBruno's's health,
Thattilat all this day the sluggard keepskeepes his bed.
See, see his window's ope;, we'llwe'l call to him.
What hoe, BenvolioBenuolio!
sp.stage:
Enter BenvolioBenuolio aboveaboue at a window, in his
nightcap,: buttoning.

sp.l: What a devildeuill ailayle you two?
Speak softly, sir, lestleast the devildeuil hearheare you,:
For Faustus at the courtCourt is late arrivedarriu'd,
And at his heelsheeles a thousand furies waitwaite,
To accomplish whatsoeverwhat soeuevr the Doctor please.
What of this?
Come leaveleaue thy chamber first, and thou shalt see
This conjurorConiurer performperforme such rare exploits,
Before the Pope andsnd royalroyall EmperorEmperour,
As neverneuer yet was seenseene in Germany.
Has not the Pope enough of conjuringconiuring yet?
He was uponvpon the devil'sdeuils backbacke late enough,;
And if he be so farfarre in loveloue with him,
I would he would post with him to Rome againagaine.
SpeakSpeake, wilt thou come and see this sport?
Not I.
Wilt thou stand in thy windowWindow, and see it then?
AyI, and I fall not asleepasleepe i'th'meani'th meane time.
The EmperorEmperour is at hand;, who comes to see
What wonders by blackblacke spellsspels may compass be.

sp.p:
Well, go you attend the EmperorEmperour.: I am content
for this once to thrust my head out at a window,: for they say,
if a man be drunkdrunke overnightouer, thenight, Devilthe cannotDiuell hurtcannot himhurt inhim in
the morning.: Ifif that bebee true, I havehaue a charmcharme in my head,
shall controlcontroule him as well as the conjurorConiurer, I warrant you.

div.stage:
A sennetSenit. Charles the GermanGermane EmperorEmperour, Bruno
Saxony, Faustus, MephistophilisMephostophilis, Frede-
rickricke, Martino, and Atten-
dants.

sp.l: Wonder of men, renownedrenown'd magicianMagitian,
Thrice-learnedThrice learned Faustus, welcome to our courtCourt
This deed of thine, in setting Bruno free
From his and our professed enemy,
Shall addadde more excellence untovnto thine artArt,
ThanThen if by powerfulpowerfull necromanticNecromantick spellsspels,
Thou could'stcouldst command the world'sworlds obedience,:
ForeverFor euer be belovedbelou'd of Carolus.
And if this Bruno thou hast late redeemedredeem'd,
In peace possesspossesse the triple diademDiadem,
And sit in PeterPeters's chairChaire, despite of chance,
Thou shalt be famous through all Italy,
And honouredhonour'd of the GermanGermane EmperorEmperour.
These gracious words, most royalroyall Carolus,
Shall make poorpoore Faustus to his utmostvtmost power,
Both loveloue and serveserue the GermanGermane EmperorEmperour,
And lay his life at holy BrunoBruno's's feet.
For proofproofe whereof, if so your Grace be pleasedpleas'd,
The Doctor stands preparedprepar'd, by power of artArt,
To cast his magicMagicke charmscharmes, that shall pierce through
The ebon'Ebon gates of ever-burningeuer-burning hell,
And hailhale the stubbornstubborne Furies from their cavescaues,
To compasscompasse whatsoe'erwhatsoere your grace commands.

sp.p:
BloodBloud, he speaksspeakes terribly,: but for all that, I dodoe not
greatly believebeleeue him;, he lookslookes as like conjurorConiurer as the Pope to a coster-Coster-
monger.

sp.l: Then, Faustus, as thou late did'stdidst promise usvs
We would behold that famous conquerourConquerour,
Great Alexander, and his paramourParamour,
In their true shapes, and state majesticalMaiesticall,
That we may wonder at their excellence.
Your majestyMaiesty shall see them presently.,
sp.p: MephistophilisMephosto, away.
sp.l: And with a solemnsolemne noisenoyse of trumpets sound,
Present before this royalroyall EmperorEmperour,
Great Alexander and his beauteous paramourParamour.

sp.p: Faustus, I will.

Well, MasterM. Doctor, an your devilsDiuels come not away
quickly, havehaue me asleepasleepe presently.: Zoundszounds, I could
eateate my anger, to thinkthinke I havehaue beenbeene such an assAsse
all this stand gaping after the devil'sdiuels governorGouernor, and
can see nothing.

I'llIl'e make you feelfeele something anon, if my artArt failfaile
me not.
sp.l: My Lord, I must forewarnforewarne your majestyMaiesty,
That when my spiritsSpirits present the royalroyall shapes
Of Alexander and his paramourParamour,
Your grace demand no questions of the King,
But in dumbdumbe silence let them come and gogoe.
Be it as Faustus please;, we are content.

sp.p:
AyI, ayI, and I am content too,: and thou bring Alex-
ander and his paramourParamour before the EmperorEmperour., I'llIl'e be Acte-
on, and turnturne myselfmy selfe to a stagStagge.

And I'llIl'e play Diana, and send you the hornshornes pre-
sently.

SennetSenit. Enter at one the EmperorEmperour Alexander, at the other

der killskils him,; takes off his crownCrowne, and offering to gogoe

brace them, which Faustus seeing,
suddenly staysstaies him. Then, trum-
pets cease, and musicMusicke
sounds.

sp.l: My gracious lordLord, you dodoe forget yourselfyour selfe;
These are but shadowsshadowes, not substantialsubstantiall.
O, pardon me, my thoughts are so ravishedrauished
With sight of this renowned EmperorEmperour,
That in mine armsarmes I would havehaue compassedcompast him.
But, Faustus, since I may not speakspeake to them,
To satisfysatisfie my longing thoughts at full,
Let me this tell thee: I havehaue heard it said,
That this fairfaire ladyLady, whil'stwhilest she livedliu'd on earth,
Had on her necknecke a little wart, or mole.;
How may I proveproue that saying to be true?
Your MajestyMaiesty may boldly gogoe and see.
Faustus, I see it plainplaine,
And in this sight thou better pleasest me,
ThanThen if I gainedgain'd another monarchyMonarchie.
Away, be gone. Exit Show.
sp.p:
See, see, my gracious lordLord, what strange beast is yon, that
thrusts his head out at window.

sp.speaker: EmperorEmp.
sp.l: O, wondrous sight.: Seesee, Duke of Saxony,
Two spreading hornshornes most strangely fastened
Upon the head of youngyong Benvolio.
What, is he asleepasleepe?, Oror dead?
He sleeps, my lordLord, but dreamsdreames not of his hornshornes.

sp.p:
This sport is excellent.: We'llwee'l call and wake him.
What ho, Benvolio!.

sp.l: A plague uponvpon you!, Letlet me sleepsleepe a while.

sp.p:
I blame thee not to sleepsleepe much, havinghauing such a head
of thine ownowne.

sp.l: LookLooke upvp, Benvolio, 'tistis the EmperorEmperour calls.
The EmperorEmperour? Wherewhere? O, zounds, my head.

sp.p:
Nay, and thy horns hold;, notis matterno formatter thyfor thy
head, for that's armedarm'd sufficiently.

Why, how now, sir Knight?, Whatwhat, hangedhang'd by the
horns? Thisthis most horrible!: Fiefie, fie, pull in your head for shame;,
let not all the world wonder at you.

sp.l: Zounds, Doctor, is this your villainyvillany?
O, say not so, sir.: Thethe Doctor has no skill,
No artArt, no cunning, to present these lordsLords,
Or bring before this royalroyall EmperorEmperour
The mightymightie monarchMonarch, warlikewarlicke Alexander.
If Faustus do it, you are straightstreight resolvedresolu'd,
In bold Acteon'sActeons shape to turnturne a stagStagge.
And therefore, my lordLord, so please your majestyMaiesty,
I'llI'le raise a kennelkennelll of houndsHounds shall hunt him so
As all his footmanship shall scarce prevailpreuaile,
To keepkeepe his carcassCarkasse from their bloodybloudy fangsphangs.
Ho, Belimote, Argiron, Asterote.

sp.p:
Hold, hold!: Zoundszounds, he'llhee'l raise upvp a kennelkennell of devilsDiuels,
I thinkthinke anon.: Goodgood, my lordLord, entreatintreate for me.: 'Sblood'sbloud, I am
neverneuer able to endure these torments.

sp.l: Then good MasterM. Doctor,
Let me entreatintreate you to removeremoue his hornshornes;,
He has done penance now sufficiently.

sp.p:
My gracious Lord, not so much for injuryiniury done to
me, as to delight your majestyMaiesty with some mirth: hath Faustus
justlyiustly requited this injuriousiniurious knightknight', which being all I de-
sire, I am content to removeremoue his hornshornes. MephistophilisMephastophilis,
transformtransforme him,;
and hereafter, sir, looklooke you speakspeake well of
scholarsSchollers.

SpeakSpeake well of yeyee? 'Sblood'sbloud, and scholarsSchollers be such
cuckold-makersCuckold-makers to clap hornshornes of honest men'smens headsheades o'this
order;, I'llIl'e ne'ernere trust smooth faces, and small ruffsruffes more. But,
an I be not revengedreueng'd for this, would I might be turnedturn'd to a
gaping oysterOyster, and drinkdrinke nothing but salt water.

sp.l: Come, Faustus, whilewhle the EmperorEmperour livesliues,
In recompenserecompence of this thy high desert,
Thou shalt command the state of Germany,
And liveliue belovedbelou'd of mightymightie Carolus. Exeunt omnes.

div.stage:
Enter Benvolio, Martino, FrederickFredericke, and
SoldiersSouldiers.

sp.l: Nay, sweet Benvolio, let usvs sway thy thoughts
From this attempt against the conjurorConiurer.
Away, you loveloue me not, to urgevrge me thus,
Shall I let slip so great an injury,
When everyeuery servileseruile groomgroome feasts at my wrongs,
And in their rusticrusticke gambolsgambals proudly say,
BenvolioBenvolio's's head was gracedgrac't with hornshornes to day?
O, may these eyelidseye-lids neverneuer close againagaine,
Till with my sword I havehaue that conjurorConiurer slainslaine.
If you will aid me in this enterprise,
Then draw your weapons, and be resolute.:
If not, depart.: Herehere will Benvolio die,
But Faustus' death shall quit my infamyinfamie.
Nay, we will stay with thee;, betide what may,
And kill that Doctor if he come this way.
Then, gentle FrederickFredericke, hie thee to the grovegroue,
And place our servantsseruants, and our followers
Closeclose in an ambush there behindbehinde the trees.,
By this (I know) the conjurorConiurer is nearneere;,
I saw him kneelkneele, and kisskisse the EmperorEmperours's hand,
And take his leaveleaue, laden with rich rewards.
Then souldiersSouldiers boldly fight.; Ifif Faustus die,
Take you the wealth;, leaveleaue usvs the victoryvictorie.
Come soldierssouldiers, follow me untovnto the grovegroue.,
Who killskils him shall havehaue gold, and endlessendlesse loveloue.
My head is lighter thanthen it was by th'hornsth'hornes,
But yet my heart more ponderous then my head,
And pants untilvntill I see that conjurorConiurer dead.
Where shall we place ourselvesour selues, Benvolio?
Here will we stay to bide the first assault.,
O, were that damned hell-houndHell-hound but in place,
Thou soonsoone should'stshouldst see me quit my foulfoule disgrace.
Close, close, the conjurorConiurer is at hand,
And all alone, comes walking in his gowngowne;
Be ready, then, and strike the peasantPeasant downdowne.
Mine be that honour then.: Nownow, sword, strike home.,
For hornshornes he gavegaue, I'llIl'e havehaue his head anonanone.

div.stage: Enter Faustus with the false head.

sp.l: See, see, he comes.
No words.: Thisthis blow ends all.,
Hell take his soulsoule;, his body thus must fall.
Oh!.
GroanGrone you, Master Doctor?
BreakBreake may his heart with gropes.: Deardeere FrederickFrederik, see
Thus will I end his griefsgriefes immediatelyimmediatly.
Strike with a willing hand;, his head is off.
The devil'sDiuel's dead;, the Furies now may laugh.
Was this that sternsterne aspect, that awfulawfull frownfrowne,
Made the grim monarch of infernalinfernall spirits,
Tremble and quake at his commanding charmscharmes?
Was this that damned head, whose heart conspiredconspir'd
BenvolioBenvolio's's shame before the EmperorEmperour?.
AyI, that's the head, and here the body lies,
JustlyIustly rewarded for his villainiesvillanies.
Come, let's devisedeuise how we may addadde more shame
to the blackblacke scandalscandall of his hated name.
First, on his head, in quittance of my wrongs,
I'llIl'e nailnaile huge forked hornshornes, and let them hang
Within the window where he yokedyoak'd me first,
That all the world may see my justiust revengereuenge.

sp.p: What usevse shall we put his beard to?

We'llWee'l sell it to a chimney-sweeperChimny-sweeper.: Itit will wearweare out
ten birchingbirchin broomsbroomes, I warrant you.
What shall eyes dodoe?

We'llWee'l put out his eyes, and they shall serveserue for but-
tons to his lips, to keepkeepe his tongue from catching cold.

An excellent policypolicie.: Andand now, sirs, havinghauing divideddiuided
him, what shall the body dodoe?
Zounds, the devil'sDiuel's alivealiue againagen!.
GiveGiue him his head, for God'sGods sake.

sp.l: Nay, keepkeepe it.: Faustus will havehaue heads and hands.,
I call your hearts to recompenserecompence this deed.
Knew you not, traitorsTraytors, I was limitedlimitted
For fourfoure and twenty yearsyeares, to breathe on earth?
And had you cut my body with your swords,
Or hewedhew'd this flesh and bones as small as sand,
Yet in a minute had my spirit returnedreturn'd,
And I had breathedbreath'd a man made free from harmharme.
But wherefore dodoe I dally my revengereuenge?
Asteroth, Belimoth, MephistophilisMephostophilis,
Go horse these traitorstraytors on your fireyfiery backsbackes, Enter Meph. & other Deuils.
And mount aloft with them as high as heavenheauen;,
Thence pitch them headlong to the lowest hell.:
Yet stay, the world shall see their miserymiserie,
And hell shall after plague their treacherytreacherie.
Go, Belimothe, and take this caitiffcaitife hence,
And hurlhurle him in some lake of mud and dirtdurt.:
Take thou this other;, dragdragge him through the woods,
Among'stAmongst the pricking thornsthornes, and sharpest briarsbriers,
Whil'stWhilst with my gentle MephistophilisMephostophilis,
This traitorTraytor flies untovnto some steepsteepie rockrocke,
That rollingrowling downdowne, may breakbreake the villain'svillaines bones,
As he intended to dismember me.
Fly hence, dispatch my charge immediatelyimmediatly.
PityPitie usvs, gentle Faustus;, savesaue our livesliues.,
Away.
He must needs gogoe that the devilDiuell drivesdriues.

sp.speaker: 1 SoldierSold.
sp.l: Come, sirs, prepare your sellsselues in readinessreadinesse;,
Make hastehast to help these noble gentlemenGentlemen.,
I heard them parleyparry with the conjurorConiurer.

What's here? Anan ambush to betray my life!:
Then, Faustus, try thy skill.: Basebase peasantspesants, stand.,
For loloe, these treesTrees removeremoue at my command,
And stand as bulwarksBulwarkes 'twixttwixt yourselvesyour selues and me,
To shieldsheild me from your hated treachery.:
Yet to encounter this your weakweake attempt,
Behold an armyArmy comes incontinent.
