sp.l: What ho, BenvolioBenuolio.
Here, what Frederick, ho.

sp.p: O help me, gentle friend; where is Martino?

sp.l: DearDeere Frederick, here,
HalfHalfe smotheredsmother'd in a lakeLake of mud and dirtdurt,
Through which the Furies draggeddrag'd me by the heelsheeles.

sp.speaker: Fred.
sp.l: Martino, see,
Benvolio'sBenuolio's hornshornes againagaine.
O misery!, Howhow now, BenvolioBenuolio?
Defend me, heavenheauen., Shallshall I be haunted still?
Nay, fearfeare not, man; we havehaue no power to kill.
My friends transformed thus.: O hellish spite!, Your
heads are all set with hornshornes.
You hit it right;,
It is your ownowne you meanmeane. Feelfeele on your head.
'Zounds'Zons, hornshornes againagaine!.
Nay, chafe not man;, we all are sped.
What devildeuill attends this damneddamn'd magicianMagician,
That, spite of spite, our wrongs are doubled?
What may we do, that we may hide our shames?
If we should follow him to workworke revengereuenge,
He'd joinioyne long asses'Asses earseares to these huge hornshornes,
And make usvs laughing stocksstockes to all the world.
What shall we then do, deardeere BenvolioBenuolio?
I havehaue a castleCastle joiningioyning nearneere these woods,
And thither we'llwee'le repairrepaire and liveliue obscure,
Till time shall alter this our brutish shapes.:
SinceSith blackblacke disgrace hath thus eclipsedeclipst our fame,.
We'llWe'le rather die with griefgriefe, thanthen liveliue with shame.

div.stage:
Enter Faustus, and the Horse-courser, and
MephistophilisMephostophilis.

sp.p:
I beseech your worshipWorship, accept of these forty
dollarsDollors.

Friend, thou can'stcanst not buy so good a horse, for so
small a price.: I havehaue no great need to sell him, but if thou
lik'stlikest him for ten dollarsDollors more, take him, because I see thou
hast a good mindminde to him.

I beseech you, sir, accept of this; I am a very poorpoore
man, and havehaue lost very much of late by horse flesh, and this
bargainbargaine will set me upvp againagaine.

Well, I will not stand with thee., Givegiue me the mo-
ney.: Nownow, sirrahsirra, I must tell you, that you may ride him o'ero're
hedge and ditch, and spare him not,; but do you hearheare? Inin any
case, ride him not into the water.

How, sir, not into the water? Whywhy will he not drink
of all waters?

Yes, he will drinkdrinke of all waters, but ride him not
into the water.; O'ero're hedge and ditch, or where thou wilt, but
not into the water.: Go bid the hostlerHostler deliverdeliuer him untovnto you,
and remember what I say.

I warrant you, sir,; O joyfulioyfull day,: nowNow am I a
made man foreverfor euer. Exit.

sp.l: What art thou, Faustus, but a man condemnedcondemn'd to die?
Thy fatalfatall time drawsdrawes to a finalfinall end.
DespairDespaire doth drivedriue distrust into my thoughts.
Confound these passions with a quiet sleepsleepe.
Tush, Christ did call the thiefTheefe uponvpon the crossCrosse;
Then rest thee, Faustus, quiet in conceit.

sp.p:
O, what a cozeningcosening Doctor was this? I, riding my
horse into the water, thinking some hidden mystery had beenbeene
in the horse, I had nothing undervnder me but a little straw, and
had much ado to escape drowning.: Well, I'llI'le go rouse him,
and make him givegiue me my forty dollarsDollors againagaine. Ho, sirrahsirra
Doctor, you cozeningcosoning scab.; MasterMaister Doctor, awake, and rise,
and givegiue me my moneymony againagaine, for your horse is turned to a
bottle of hayHay, — MasterMaister Doctor.
He pullspuls off his leg.
Alas, I am undonevndone;, what shall I do?
I havehaue pulledpuld off his leg.
O, help, help, the villainvillaine hath murderedmurder'd me!.

Murder or not murder, now he has but one leg.,
I'llI'le out-run him, and cast this leg into some ditch or other.

sp.speaker: Faustus
sp.p:
Stop him, stop him, stop him!— ha, ha, ha! Faus-
stus hath his leg againagaine, and the Horse-courser a bundle of hay
for his forty dollarsDollors.
Enter Wagner.
How now, Wagner, what newsnewes with thee?

If it please you, the Duke of Vanholt doth earnest-
ly entreatentreate your company, and hath sent some of his men to
attend you with provisionproutston fit for your journeyiourney.

div.stage: Enter ClownClowne, Dick, Horse-courser, and a Carter.

sp.p:
Come, my mastersMaisters, I'llI'le bring you to the best beerbeere
in Europe., Whatwhat ho, HostessHostis; where be these whoresWhores?

How now, what lacklacke you? What, my old guestsGuesse,
welcome.
SirrahSirra Dick, dost thou know why I stand so mute?

sp.speaker: Dick.
sp.p: No, Robin, why is't?

I am eighteeneighteene pence on the score, but say nothing.,
Seesee if she havehaue forgotten me.

Who's this, that stands so solemnly by himselfhimselfe?:
Whatwhat, my old guestGuest?
O, HostessHostisse, how do you? I hope my score stands still.

Ay ,I there's no doubt of that, for me thinksthinkes you make
no hastehast to wipe it out.

sp.speaker: Dick.
sp.p: Why, HostessHostesse, I say, fetch usvs some beerBeere.
You shall presently.: Looklooke upvp into th'hall there, ho!. Exit.

sp.speaker: Dick.
sp.p:
Come, sirs, what shall we do now till mine hostessHostesse
comes?

Marry, sir, I'llI'le tell you the bravestbrauest tale how a con-Con-
juroriurer servedseru'd me.; Youyou know Doctor FaustusFauster?.

Ay I, a plague take him., Here'sheere's some on's havehaue cause
to know him.; Diddid he conjureconiure thee too?

I'llI'le tell you how he servedseru'd me.: As I was going to
WittenbergWittenberge th'othert'other day, with a loadloade of hayHay, he met me,
and asked me what he should givegiue me for as much hayHay as he
could eateate.; Nownow, sir, I, thinking that a little would serveserue his
turnturne, bad him take as much as he would for threethree-farthings; farthings.
Soso he presently gavegaue me my moneymony, and fell to eating,; and as I
am a cursen man, he neverneuer left eating, till he had eateate upvp all
my loadloade of hay.

sp.speaker: All.
sp.p: O monstrous!, Eateate a whole load of hayHay?!

Yes, yes, that may be,; for I havehaue heard of one, that
hasha's eateate a load of logslogges.

Now, sirs, you shall hearheare how villainouslyvillanously he servedseru'd
memee.: I went to him yesterday to buy a horse of him, and he
wouldWould by no meansmeanes sell him undervnder forty40 dollarsDollors.; Soso, sir, because
I knew him to be such a horse, as would run overouer hedge and
ditch, and neverneuer tiretyre, I gavegaue him his money.; Soso when I had
my horse, Doctor FaustusFauster bad me ride him night and day, and
spare him no time.; Butbut, quoth he, in any case ride him not in-
to the water. Now, sir, I thinking the horse had had some
quality that he would not havehaue me know of, what did I but
rid him into a great riverriuer, and when I came justiust in the midst
my horse vanishedvanisht away, and I satsate straddling uponvpon a bottle
of hayHay.

sp.speaker: All.
sp.p: O, bravebraue Doctor!.

But you shall hearheare how bravelybrauely I servedseru'd him for
it; I went me home to his house, and there I found him
asleepasleepe.; I kept a hallowing and whooping in his earseares, but
all could not wake him.: I, seeing that, tooktooke him by the leg,
and neverneuer rested pulling, till I had pulledpul'd me his leg quite off,
and now 'tis at home in mine hostryHostry.

And has the Doctor but one leg then? That'sthat's excel-
lent, for one of his devilsdeuils turnedturn'd me, into the likenesslikenesse of an
ape'sApes face.
Some more drinkdrinke, HostessHostesse.

HarkHearke you, we'llwe'le into another roomroome and drinkdrinke
a while, and then we'llwe'le go seekseeke out the Doctor.

sp.speaker: Duke.of Vanholt
sp.p:
ThanksThankes MasterMaister Doctor, for these pleasant sights.,
Nor know I how sufficiently to recompenserecompence your great de-
serts in erecting that enchantedinchanted castleCastle in the airAire,: the
sightSight whereof so delighted me,
asAs nothing in the world could please me more.

. I do thinkthinke myselfmy, myselfe goodmy lordgood, highlyLord, recom-highly recom-
pensedpenced, in that it pleaseth your grace to thinkthinke but well of
that which Faustus hath performed. But, graciousgratious ladyLady, it

Thereforetherefor, I pray you tell me, what is the thing you most de-

dainty.

True, MasterMaister Doctor, and since I findfinde you so kind,

time of the winterWinter, I would request no better meatmeate, thanthen

This is but a small matter.: Go, MephostophilisMephostophilis, away.
Exit MephistophilisMephosto.
Madam, I will do more thanthen this for your content.
Enter MephistophilisMepho. againagen with the grapes.
Here, now taste yeyee these., Theythey should be good
For they come from a farfarre countryCountry, I can tell you.

sp.speaker: Duke .of Vanholt
sp.p:
This makes me wonder more thanthen all the rest, that
at this time of the yearyeare, when everyeuery treeTree is barren of his
fruitfruite, from whence you had these ripe grapes.

Please it, your grace, the yearyeare is divideddiuided into two
circles overouer the whole world, so that when it is winterWinter with
usvs, in the contrary circle it is likewise summerSummer with them,
as in India, Saba, and such countriesCountries that lielye farfarre eastEast,
where they havehaue fruit twice a yearyeare,. fromFrom whence, by meansmeanes
of a swift spirit that I havehaue, I had these grapes brought as
you see.

sp.speaker: Lady
sp.p:
And trust me, they are the sweetest grapes that
e'ere're I tasted.

div.stage: The ClownsClowne bounce at the gate, within.

sp.l: What rude disturbers havehaue we at the gate ?
Go, pacifypacifie their fury. Setset it ope,

Why, how now, mastersMaisters?, Whatwhat a coilcoyle is
there?
What is the reason you disturbdisturbe the Duke?

sp.speaker: Dick.

Why, saucy varlets, dare you be so bold?.

I hope, sir, we havehaue wit enough to be more bold
thanthen welcome.

It appearsappeares so., Praypray be bold elsewhereelse-where,
And trouble not the Duke.

sp.speaker: Duke .of Vanholt
sp.p: What would they havehaue?
They all cry out to speakspeake with Doctor Faustus.
AyI, and we will speakspeake with him.,

sp.p:
Commit with usvs!, Hehe were as good commit with his

They are good subject for a merriment.

I thankthanke your grace.:
Enter theClown Clowne, Dick, Carter, and

Why, how now, my goods friends?
'Faith you are too outrageousoutragious, but come nearneere.

zensen of beerBeere here, and be hangedhang'd.
Nay, harkhearke you, can you tell me where you are?
AyI, marry can I., Wewe are undervnder heavenheauen.
AyI, but, sir sauce-boxsauce box know you in what place?

Ay I, ayI, the house is good enough to drink in.: ZoundsZons,
fill usvs some beerBeere, or we'll breakbreake all the barrels in the househou, se,
and dash out all your brainsbraines with your bottlesBottles.

sp.l: Be not so furious.: Comecome, you shall havehaue beerBeere.
My lordLord, beseech you givegiue me leaveleaue awhilea while.
I'llI'le gage my credit;, 'twill content your grace.

sp.speaker: Duke .of Vanholt
sp.l: With all my heart, kind Doctor, please thyselfthy selfe,
Our servantsseruants, and our courtsCourts at thy command.

sp.p:
I humbly thankthanke your grace.: Thenthen fetch some
beerBeere.

AyI, marrymary., Therethere spake a Doctor indeed, and 'faith I'llIle

My woodenwoodden leg? Whatwhat dost thou meanmeane by that?

Ha, ha, ha!, Dostdost hearheare him Dick?, Hehe has forgot his

AyI, ayI,. he does not stand much uponvpon that.
No, faith., Notnot much uponvpon a woodenwoodden leg.

Good Lord, that flesh and bloodbloud should be so frailfraile
with your worshipWorship.: Do not you remember a horse-courserHorse-courser
you sold a horse to?
Yes, I remember I sold one a horse.

And do you remember you bid he should not ride
into the water?
Yes, I do veryverie well remember that.
And do you remember nothing of your leg?
No, in good sooth.
Then I pray remember your courtesycurtesie.
I thank you, sir.
'Tis not so much worth.; I pray you, telltel me one thing.
What's that?
Be both your legs bedfellowsbedfellowes everyeuery night together?

Would'stWouldst thou make a colossusColossus of me, that thou as-
kest me such questions?

No, trulytruelie, sir, I would make nothing of you, but
I would fainfaine know that.
Enter HostessHostesse with drinkdrinke.
Then I assure thee certainlycertainelie they are.
I thankthanke you;, I am fully satisfied.
But wherefore dost thou askaske?

For nothing, sir,: but methinksme youthinkes shouldyou haveshould ahaue a

Why do you hearheare, sir?, Diddid not I pull off one of your

But I havehaue it againagaine now I am awake.: Looklooke you

sp.speaker: All.
sp.p: O horrible!, Hadhad the Doctor three legs?

Do you remember, sir, how you cozenedcosened me and eat

sp.p:
Do you remember how you made me wearweare an
ape'sApes ---

You whoreson conjuringconiuring scab, do you remember

Who payspayes for the aleAle? Hearheare you, MasterMaister Doctor,

sp.speaker: Lady.
sp.p:
My Lord,
weWe are much beholding to this learned man.

sp.speaker: Duke .of Vanholt

With all the loveloue and kindnesskindnesse that we may.
His artfulArtfull sport, drivesdriues all sad thoughts away. Exeunt.

div.stage:
Thunder and lightning.: Enter devilsdeuils with coveredcouer'd

Faustus' studyStudy.: Then enter
Wagner.

sp.p:
I think my masterMaister means to die shortly., Hehe hath made
his will, and& givengiuen me hish is wealth, his house, his goods, and& store of
golden plate,; besides two thousand ducatsduckets ready coinedcoin'd.:
I
wonder what he meansmeanes., Ifif death were nie, he would not fro-

such belly-cheerbelly-cheere, as Wagner in his life ne'ernere saw the like.: Andand
see where they come;, belike the feast is done.
Exit.

div.stage:
Enter Faustus, MephistophilisMephostophilis, and two or three
ScholarsSchollers.

sp.speaker: 1. ScholarSchol.
sp.p:
MasterM. Doctor Faustus, since our conference about
fairfaire ladiesLadies, which was the beautifullest in all the world, we
havehaue determineddetermin'd with ourselvesour selues that Helen of Greece was
the admirablest ladyLady that evereuer livedliu'd.: Thereforetherefore, MasterM. Doctor, if
you will dodoe usvs so much favorfauour, as to let usvs see that peerlesspeerelesse
dame of Greece, whom all the world admires for majestyMaiesty, we
should thinkthinke ourselvesour selues much beholding untovunto you.

sp.l: Gentlemen, for thatyt I know your friendship is unfeignedvnfain'd,
It is not Faustus' customcustome to deny
The justiust request of those that wish him well.:
You shall behold that peerlesspeerelesse dame of Greece,
No otherwise for pomppompe or majestyMaiesty,
ThanThen when Sirsir Paris cross the seas with her,
And brought the spoilsspoyles to rich Dardania.:
Be silent then, for danger is in words.

div.stage:
MusicMusicke soundssound., MephistophilisMephosto brings in HelenHellen;, she passeth
overouer the stage.

sp.l: Was this fairfaire Helen whose admired worth
Made Greece with ten yearsyeares warswarres afflictafslict poorpoore Troy?
Too simple is my wit to tell her worth,
Whom all the world admires for majesty.
Now we havehaue seenseene the pride of nature'sNatures workworke,
We'llWee'l take our leavesleaues, and for this blessed sight
Happy and blest be Faustus evermoreeuermore. Exeunt ScholarsSchollers.
Gentlemen, farewell;: the same wish I to you.

sp.speaker: Old Man.
sp.l: O, gentle Faustus, leaveleaue this damned artArt,
This magicMagicke, that will charmcharme thy soulsoule to hell,
And quite bereavebereaue thee of salvationsaluation.
Though thou hast now offended like a man,
DoDoe not perseverperseuer in it like a devilDiuell.;
Yet, yet, thou hast an amiable soulsoule,
If sin by customcustome grow not into nature;:
Then, Faustus, will repentance come too late,

Now hast thou but one bare hourhoure to liveliue,
